coredata.dc:description: A new type of green carbon quantum dots (ILB-CQDs) was prepared by hydrothermal method using ionic liquid as a modifier and grape skin as carbon source, and was obtained from hydrogen-bonded lattice structure ionic liquid preparation, which makes the CQDs in a ring-like stable structure with a stability period of more than 90 day. There is also the catalytic effect of the ionic liquid on cellulose, which makes the prepared CQDs show good advantages, such as uniform particle size, high quantum yield (26.7%), and very good fluorescence performance. This is a smart material for the selective detection of Fe3+ and Pd2+. It has a detection limit of 0.001 nM for Fe3+ and 0.23 µM for Pd2+ in pure water. It has a detection limit of 3.2 nmol/L for Fe3+ and 0.36 µmol/L for Pd2+ in actual water, both of which meet the requirements of WHO drinking water standards. And there is to achieve more than 90% of water restoration effect.

head.ce:title: Preparation of carbon quantum dots from ionic liquid modified biomass for the detection of Fe3+ and Pd2+ in environmental water

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new type of green carbon quantum dots (ILB-CQDs) was prepared by hydrothermal method using ionic liquid as a modifier and grape skin as carbon source, and was obtained from hydrogen-bonded lattice structure ionic liquid preparation, which makes the CQDs in a ring-like stable structure with a stability period of more than 90 day. There is also the catalytic effect of the ionic liquid on cellulose, which makes the prepared CQDs show good advantages, such as uniform particle size, high quantum yield (26.7%), and very good fluorescence performance. This is a smart material for the selective detection of Fe3+ and Pd2+. It has a detection limit of 0.001 nM for Fe3+ and 0.23 µM for Pd2+ in pure water. It has a detection limit of 3.2 nmol/L for Fe3+ and 0.36 µmol/L for Pd2+ in actual water, both of which meet the requirements of WHO drinking water standards. And there is to achieve more than 90% of water restoration effect.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Biomass ionic liquid smart materials fluorescent probe

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new type of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, which consist of dispersed spherical carbon particles. With sizes ranging from 2 to 10 nm, they have attracted a lot of attention over the years due to their excellent properties. The earliest date can be traced back to 2004 when Xu et al. stumbled upon luminescent nanoparticles in the process of preparing carbon (Xiaoyou Xu et al., 2004), which were first noticed for their fluorescent properties. In 2011, to explore a systematic research system for the preparation of CQDs, specially prepared with organic solvents, Li et al. discovered the property that carbon nanoparticles fluoresce when irradiated by excitation light, which has attracted the attention of many researchers (Li et al., 2011). In addition to the optical properties, biomass as a carbon source brings a more extensive and inexpensive preparation method, and in 2013, Hung et al. obtained CQDs using biomass as a carbon source (Huang et al., 2013), which has good water solubility and biocompatibility, further promoting the development of CQDs preparation. As a member of the carbon material family, CQDs exhibit excellent properties, including strong fluorescence intensity, good electrical conductivity, and high stability, making them moldable and promising for applications, especially in fluorescent detection probes (Omer et al., 2019), cell imaging (Cao et al., 2007), and LED devices (Cui et al., 2018), and so on.

Nowadays, the preparation methods of CQDs mainly include chemical oxidation (Wu et al., 2014), hydrothermal method (Gu et al., 2015), microwave method (Zhu et al., 2009), and laser needle lithography (Li et al., 2011). In contrast, our chosen hydrothermal method is not only simple to prepare, but also allows us to control the conditions of the reaction, which is a more mature preparation method. For the selection of carbon source materials, considering the source, cost, and environmental friendliness, based on previous studies, we selected green waste biomass as a carbon source. These studies reported the preparation of CQD using plant organs roots (Liu et al., 2017), stems (Wei et al., 2019), leaves (Bano et al., 2018), flowers (Shi et al., 2015), fruits (Gong et al., 2015), and seeds(Marouzi et al., 2021). The use of biomass as a carbon source faces some challenges while being environmentally friendly. For example, low quantum yield (QY) and fluorescence intensity. A review of the literature revealed that the pretreatment of biomass can improve the performance of carbon quantum dots (Mehta et al., 2015). For example, first charring the watermelon rind as a carbon source at a low temperature and evaporating the excess water in it resulted in a quantum yield of 7.1% (Zhou et al., 2012). It can be seen that carbonization is an effective treatment. Later, better performance was achieved by adding auxiliary solvents. For example, the addition of sulfuric acid and urea as catalysts during the reaction was found to increase the carbon content from 47.4% to 88.2% (Jiang et al., 2018). There is also the addition of nitrogen dopant ethylenediamine to incorporate nitrogen-containing groups, which makes the fluorescence enhanced (Xiong et al., 2022). Through the study of this literature, we have used ionic liquids(ILs) as dopants by using their very strong solubility (Zhuo et al., 2017), as mediators (Malhotra, 2007) and catalysts (Swatloski et al., 2002) in the reactions. Due to the accelerated decomposition of cellulose in biomass by ionic liquids, the biomass was more fully utilized in the reaction and the reaction time was greatly reduced. The final results also proved that the quantum yield was improved, the nitrogen doping was accomplished without the addition of other reagents, and the fluorescence properties were enhanced (Liu and Zhu, 2019).

After a study of biomass and ionic liquid literature, grape skins were selected as a carbon source, which is natural and recyclable as biomass waste. There is a large amount of carbon-containing material in it, which can be turned into fluorescent CQDs after a hydrothermal reaction. The shortcomings of biomass CQDs are also evident, with lower stability and quantum yields making them unable to meet future challenges. Therefore, we used a non-toxic, easily soluble "green" Glycolic acid: Proline ionic liquid as a modifying agent to catalyze the grape skins. It leads to enhanced conversion of carbonaceous material from biomass and thus to higher quantum yields. Glycolic acid: Proline also has a nitrogen doping effect that leads to the stronger fluorescence intensity of CQDs. It is a lattice structure by itself, and after combining with grape skin, it forms a closed-loop chemical structure, which greatly enhances the stability of CQDs, both in terms of fluorescence cycle and interference resistance. In terms of environmental protection, both are "green substances", and the prepared CQDs are non-toxic and easily soluble, so they are very friendly to the environment.

Nowadays, increasing levels of Fe3+ in environmental waters have entered people's daily lives and pose a potential threat to human health. The current traditional detection methods can no longer meet the current requirements for Fe3+ detection, so there is an urgent need to construct Fe3+ fluorescent probes with better selectivity and sensitivity. Previously, CQDs were prepared by ILs alone for Fe3+ fluorescence detection with a detection limit of 0.14 μmol/L (Fu et al., 2010), and the principle of the detection process was analyzed by FT-IR (Ren et al., 2020). There is also a detection limit of 0.13 μmol/L for Fe3+ using biomass carrots as a carbon source for the preparation of CQDs (Liu et al., 2017). The present study has good results for Fe3+ detection, but to meet the challenges of sustainable use and a green environment, we prepared a sustainable-use fluorescent material for Fe3+ detection. And unlike the quench effect, the material produces a fluorescence enhancement effect on Fe3+, which makes the detection limit even lower, has a unique selectivity. Through a survey of the literature (Maleki et al., 2019), it was found to be feasible to apply in actual water bodies.

As one of the rarest precious metals, palladium also has a wide range of applications, such as in many pharmaceuticals, electronics, batteries, and other fields. However, since food, drugs and devices produce palladium ion (Pd2+) residues in water bodies, which can seriously affect water quality and thus plant growth, and more importantly, human health, such as irritation to eyes and skin. Therefore, the use of fluorescent sensors for rapid and simple detection of Pd2+ is very popular. For example, Gao et al. reported red Carbon Dots (CDs) synthesized by hydrothermal method for the detection of Pd2+ (Gao et al., 2018). In contrast to the use of chemicals for the preparation of detection of Pd2+, Dong et al. used biomass bitter melon as a raw material for the preparation of probes for the detection of Pd2+ with a detection limit of 0.348 μmol/L (Dong et al., 2021). Therefore, the preparation of fast-response, target-selective fluorescent probes for Pd2+ detection using natural sources is still an important and challenging task. In this paper, we prepared ILB-CQDs for the detection of actual water bodies (Baghayeri et al., 2019), not only by producing complexes with Pd2+ for selective detection, but also by producing black precipitates with Pd2+ for water remediation.

By comparing with some existing literature, it is found that the existing literature on the preparation of CQDs from biomass can be improved by adding modifiers to the original basis. Through years of research on ILs, our laboratory found that by acting on biomass cellulose through ILs, it can not only play a catalytic role in the preparation process and accelerate the reaction rate, but also regulate the surface properties of CQDs and complete nitrogen doping without adding other reagents, thus improving the stability. More importantly, through research and investigation, it was found that ILs can give CQD a negative ionic response and improve QY. On this basis, ILB-CQDs have an obvious Fluorescence enhancement effect with Fe3+, and on the contrary, have an obvious quench effect on Pd2+, so they can be well used as fluorescent probe materials for specific detection of Fe3+ and Pd2+. Due to the modification of ILs, ILB-CQDs exhibit unique selectivity for Fe3+ and Pd2+, and it has detection limits as low as 0.001 nmol/L and 0.32 µmol/L, and can also accomplish the remediation of water bodies and be applied in real water bodies (Baghayeri et al., 2018).

Heptane(C7H16), K2CO3, Na2CO3, Fe(NO3)30.9H2O, ZnCl2, CoCl20.6H2O, MgSO4, CaCl2, CuSO40.5H2O, Glycolic acid(C2H4O3), Proline(C5H9NO2), PdCl2, Ethanol(C₂H₅OH), pH buffer(pH6.86 ± 0.01) solution made from phosphate and water, all chemical reagents were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Company. The grape skins in the experiment came from the North Country supermarket in Shijiazhuang, Hebei Province, and the variety was Kyoho grapes. The water used in all experiments was deionized water.

78HW-1 thermostatic magnetic stirrer, SUS-304 high-speed multifunctional pulverizer, visible fluorescence was visible on a ZF-2 triple-use UV meter. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken using an F20 TEM (Hitachi JEM2100, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were taken in standard lens mode (Waltham ThermoFischer, USA) using an ESCALAB 250Xi with Al K Alpha (hv=1340 eV) X-ray photoelectron spectrometer obtained. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were obtained by the KBr particle method using a Bruker Tensor 27 (Germany) spectrophotometer with wave numbers from 4000 to 400 cm−1. An on-site infrared spectrometer system (Mettler-Toledo, React IR 15, Switzerland) was used to record the reactions before and after chemistry throughout the experiment to analyze the reaction mechanisms and processes. Ultraviolet and visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained using a UV-6100S UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Shanghai Yuananalysis Instruments Co., Ltd.) and a 1.0 cm quartz reaction cup. Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded on an F-7100 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) using a 150 W lamp as the excitation source (EX: EM slit 5:5 and PWT Voltage400). Absolute fluorescence quantum yields were obtained using an Edinburgh FLS 980 spectrometer.

Kyoho grape skins (100 g) were dried at 160 °C for 20 min and crushed in a pulverizer for 5 min to obtain grape skins solid powder (87 g). The two solid powders of C2H4O3 and C5H9NO2 were heated at a molar ratio of 4:1 (14.51 g: 5.49 g) for 35 min at 80 °C under the protection of 30 mL of n-heptane (C
7
H
16 ), and C7H16 was evaporated under vacuum in a rotary evaporator. After cooling at room temperature for 20 min, C2H4O3:C5H9NO2 ILs (20 g) was obtained.

Grape skin powder (0.1 g) and C2H4O3:C5H9NO2 ILs (1 g) were added to a 1 L graduated cylinder, followed by 50 mL of deionized water, and after magnetic stirred for 1 h, the mixture was uniform, centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 7 min, and the supernatant was taken. Remove the large particles that have not completed the pretreatment, and then filter the remaining large and small particles with a 0.22 µm filter membrane, put the remained clear liquid in a 45 mL reaction kettle, heat it in an oven at 220 °C for 12 h, cool it to room temperature and take it out, and then centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was filtered with a 0.22 µm membrane and dialyzed in a pure water dialysis bag (Mw1000Da) for one day. The prepared ILB-CQDs were stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smart probe to detect Fe3+ and Pd2+
ce:section.ce:para: Fe3+ solution (2 mL) was added to the prepared ILB-CQDs solution (2 mL), shaken well, and left for 10 min. After thorough mixed, the fluorescence spectra were recorded and compared with the fluorescence spectra of the original solution ILB-CQDs. Add pH buffer solution (3 mL) to the ILB-CQDs solution contained metal ions shake well and leave for 10 min, observe the appearance of Fe3+ heavy metal yellow precipitate, wait until the yellow precipitate was no longer produced, took the supernatant and recorded the fluorescence spectrum. Then the above steps were repeated with the supernatant to demonstrate that ILB-CQDs can extract Fe3+.

Pd2+ was detected at room temperature. Pd2+ solution (2 mL) was added to ILB-CQDs solution (2 mL), shaken evenly, and waited for 10 min at room temperature to record the fluorescence spectra. Then leave it overnight and found the appearance of black precipitate, took the supernatant, and recorded the fluorescence spectrum. Then the above steps were repeated with the supernatant to demonstrate that ILB-CQDs could extract Pd2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Fe3+ and Pd2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water and wastewater were taken from Hebei Meixing Company and surrounding rivers, and used after pretreatment such as extraction and centrifugation. The water was mixed with different concentrations of Fe3+ and ILB-CQDs, shaken well, and then the solutions were subjected to fluorescence detection. For Fe3+ added at concentrations of 0–500 ug/L, the limit of detection observed by the naked eye was 150 ug/L. The limit of detection observed by fluorescence was estimated to be 2.1 ug/L. Both the naked eye and spectral curve limits of detection were below the maximum drinking water standard (300 μg/L) allowed by the World Health Organization (WHO). In addition, a pH buffer solution was added to the mixture. It was shown that the fluorescence of ILB-CQDs and Fe3+ was quenched after mixing and recovered after the addition of the buffer solution. In another part, the above experiments were repeated by adding different concentrations of Pd2+ and the detection limit measured by recording the fluorescence emission intensity reached 0.36 μmol/L. The recoveries of all samples, both Fe3+ and Pd2+, were more than 90%. The results can be seen in Table 1 .

It is worth noting that the effect of the fluorescence intensity of the wastewater is close to zero under the same excitation and test conditions. Therefore, the fluorescence of the wastewater does not affect the practical application, and the experimental results of this study are reliable.

After the completion of the high-temperature hydrothermal reaction, the impurities with a yellow-black color were processed to obtain a clear solution of ILB-CQDs that appeared yellow in sunlight, and then the supernatant was extracted and examined to obtain low magnification TEM images of ILB-CQDs in Fig. 1 (a), showing that they consisted of uniformly dispersed nanodots, and (b) and (c) high magnification images to further prove this view is further supported by the analysis of these three TEM images, and (d) is obtained as a histogram of the particle size distribution of ILB-CQDs, indicating that 97% of these nanodots are in the diameter range of 3–4 nm. compared with some present literature reports of 3–8 nm (Xiong et al., 2022) and 1–10 nm (Cheng et al., 2017), ILB-CQDs have a regular surface and a concentrated particle size distribution and are expected to have good and stable physical and chemical properties (Ghasemi et al., 2019).

The surface composition and elemental analysis of the synthesized ILB-CQDs were characterized by XPS. The XPS spectra of these ILB-CQDs Fig. 2(a) showed three peaks at 284.0 eV, 400.0 eV, and 530.06 eV, belonging to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively (Liu et al., 2010). A peak associated with Si2p was observed at 102.0 eV. The XPS results suggest that ILB-CQDs are mainly composed of C, N, and O, with a small amount of Si elements from trace amounts in grape skins. The C1s spectrum Fig. 2 . (b) has four peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.0 eV, 286.7 eV, and 288.9 eV belonging to C-C, C-N, C-O, and CO, respectively (Zhao et al., 2018). The N1s spectrum Fig. 2 . (c) has three peaks at 399.6 eV, 400.3 eV, and 401.7 eV belonging to the C-N-C, N-(C)3, and N-H (Fleutot et al., 2011). The O1s spectrum Fig. 2 . (d) has two peaks at 532.2 eV and 533.8 eV, belonging to CO/OH and C-O-C groups, respectively (Sevilla and Fuertes, 2009). FT-IR (S1.) confirms that the surface contains a large number of N and O functional groups, and there is a weak "shoulder peak" at 3228 cm-1, which is the stretching vibration of the methyl and methylene C-H bonds, and its absorption is not obvious due to the stretching vibration of the O-H bonds. The absorption band at 1641 cm-1 can be attributed to the CO carbonyl vibration (Bojdys et al., 2008); the absorption peak at 1621 cm-1 is caused by the stretching vibration of CN cyano or the stretching vibration of O-H hydroxyl bond of polysaccharide; the absorption spectra at 1538 cm-1 and 1433 cm-1 are characteristic absorption peaks caused by the stretching vibration of COO- of anionic carboxylate (Balachandran, 2003.4.22); the absorption peaks at 1351 cm−1 and 1303 cm−1 are characteristic. The absorption peaks at 1351 cm−1 and 1308 cm−1 are caused by C-N stretching vibrations. The analysis of the results of XPS and FT-IR revealed that the surface of ILB-CQDs contains oxygen groups carbonyl, hydroxyl, etc., and nitrogenous groups acyl, cyano, etc., which are expected to make it have many sites when interacting with other substances and exhibit unique modes of action and effects (Barman and Sadhukhan, 2012). By summarizing the characterization results of XPS and FT-IR and then comparing them by reviewing the literature (Liu et al., 2013), the chemical structure of ILB-CQDs ( Fig. 3) was projected to be closed-loop, which proves that it has strong solution stability.

As shown by the UV spectrophotometer (UV-Vis) absorption spectra in Fig. 4 ., ILB-CQDs have two strong absorption peaks at 327 nm and 359 nm, mainly caused by the n-π*and π-π*jumps in the sp2 region (Mehta et al., 2014), which also indicates the presence of CO and CC groups in ILB-CQDs (Jing Wang, 2012.04), and with grape skin, glycolic acid: proline(C₂H₄O₃:C₅H₉NO₂) liquids prepared separately, it can be found that the organic structure changes after the addition of the natural low eutectic solvent C₂H₄O₃:C₅H₉NO₂, the absorption peak wavelength shifts from 272.2 nm and 308.2–359 nm producing a red shift, and the phenomenon that the maximum absorption peak wavelength of the absorption band shifts toward the long wave direction, which is seen to increase the degree of conjugation and reduce the energy required for the leap; and the maximum The absorption peak increases from 2.1 and 2.08–2.6, and the color enhancement effect occurs, the absorption intensity increases, and the corresponding electron leap probability is larger, which is caused by the difference in the selectivity of different sizes of CQDs for light or by the different emission holes (Singh et al., 2022) on the surface of both.

After studying the comparison of the fluorescence intensity of CQDs prepared from biomass, low eutectic ionic liquids, and biomass modified with ILs, respectively ( Fig. 5), it was found that ILB-CQDs were 30% higher than IL-CQDs and 82% higher than biomass CQDs under the same conditions. The fluorescence quantum yields of the obtained carbon quantum dots were calculated. Quinine sulfate solution (Φs=54%,360 nm) was made by dissolving quinine sulfate in 0.1 mol/L H2SO4 and using it as a standard, the fluorescence quantum yield was calculated according to equation (1).
ΦS=ΦR×FS/FR ×AR/AS ×(nS/nR)2
In equation (1).
Φs and ΦR - are the fluorescence quantum yields of the sample and quinine sulfate, respectively;.
FS and FR - are the integrated fluorescence intensities of the sample and quinine sulfate, respectively;.

AS and AR - are the absorbance of the solutions at the excitation wavelengths of the sample and quinine sulfate, respectively (to minimize the reabsorption effect, the absorbance values of the measured solutions should be less than 0.05);.
ηS and ηR - are the refractive indices of the sample and quinine sulfate, respectively.

After comparison with biomass CQD, the QY of ILB-CQD increased from 9.7% to 26.7%. It was postulated from the research literature that pretreatment using ILs can reduce the crystallinity and improve the utilization of cellulose (Li et al., 2016), and ILs (C₂H₄O₃:C₅H₉NO₂) contain organic nitrogen macromolecular cations as luminescent precursors, in which the doping of nitrogen elements affects the size, complex behavior and electron/energy transfer mechanism of CQDs (Muthusankar et al., 2018), which can effectively tune the properties of carbon quantum dots themselves and improve the luminescence efficiency. Besides acting on the pretreatment, during the reaction and when carrying out the application, the new amino acid is derived from the source natural amino acids, which have strong polarity, hydrogen bonding basicity, and biocompatibility, making CQDs rich in functional groups and make them more stable when the connection between the main structure and the modified part of biomass CQDs is not very tight and may lead to breakage between each other. So C₂H₄O₃:C₅H₉NO₂ ionic liquid and grape skin work together to make fluorescence intensity improved.

For ILB-CQDs itself fluorescence period and optimal excitation wavelength were also studied, as Fig. 6 . (a) shows the photoluminescence spectra of ILB-CQDs in 90d time, it can be found that the fluorescence intensity only decreased by 4.1% in 0–90d time, which also proves that ILB-CQDs can maintain high fluorescence for a long time, the inset of which shows the fluorescence spectra of ILB-CQDs under visible light (left) and 365 nm (right) UV irradiation. The pH stability of ILB-CQDs was studied. The pH of ILB-CQDs was adjusted to 1–14 by adding dilute hydrochloric acid or sodium hydroxide, and then the fluorescence intensity was tested. The results are shown in Fig. 6(b). The fluorescence intensity gradually decreased with the increase in pH, but the fluorescence intensity did not change much, so ILB-CQDs have strong pH stability. And ILB-CQDs itself is acidic with strong fluorescence intensity, so it can be applied without pH adjustment. As shown in Table 2, ILB-CQDs are compared with other literature for QY (%), stability, and nanoparticle diameter. These excellent properties allow it to maintain a long and stable application.

As Fig. 6 . (c) shows the fluorescence spectra in the excitation wavelength range 345–465 nm, it can be seen that the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of ILB-CQDs are 385 nm and 455 nm, respectively, and when a large number of particles are excited at 385 nm, the maximum emission intensity is generated near 455 nm, producing bright blue fluorescence (De and Karak, 2013). It was hypothesized by surveying the literature that this is due to the reaction with biomass when ILs are doped as modifiers to make surface defects with different functional groups on the surface of ILB-CQDs (Liu et al., 2012b). By observing different excitation wavelengths, the fluorescence emission spectrum of ILB-CQDs was red-shifted from 460 nm to 525 nm with the increase of excitation wavelength, while the fluorescence intensity also decreased, which indicated that the fluorescence of ILB-CQDs was very dependent on the excitation wavelength, and the study of Sahu S's article found that the reason for the red-shift in the emission spectrum might lie in the ILB- CQDs size change (Sahu et al., 2012), and also from the different surface defects formed by various functional groups (C-O, CO, and C-N-C) changing to emitting states due to surface passivation after doping modifications(Li et al., 2011).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smart probe materials for detecting Fe3+and Pd2+
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility and selectivity of this new CQD material for the detection of Fe3+ and Pd2+ were investigated. Fig. 7. shows the photoluminescence spectra of the fluorescence intensity of ILB-CQDs under different conditions. In the absence of metal ions, the blank ILB-CQDs solution has an intense luminescence peak at 395 nm, while the addition of Fe3+ leads to a significant increase in fluorescence intensity and the addition of Pd2+ leads to a significant quench compared to the presence of other metal ions. Under the same conditions, the addition of other metal ions, including Ca2+, Co2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Cu2+, and Zn2+, as shown in Fig. 7, resulted in less than 20% reduction in fluorescence with the addition of Fe3+ compared to 38.4% enhancement in fluorescence with the addition of Fe3+ and 86% reduction with the addition of Pd2+, indicating that there was no significant change with the addition of other metal ions, thus ILB-CQDs are probe materials for the specificity detection of Fe3+ and Pd2+ fluorescence.

The mechanism of Fe3+ detection by ILB-CQDs was investigated by XPS and FT-IR, and it can be seen from S2. (a) that Fe3+ was successfully introduced into ILB-CQDs, and then a strong interaction between Fe3+ and ILB-CQDs surface groups increased the radiative leap from the electron to the ground state (Li-Shing Huang, 2001.12.7), completing the electron-hole pair complex, and from Fig. 2. and S2. of the XPS spectra, it can be seen that the N content increases significantly before and after the reaction of adding Fe3+, and Fe3+ has a higher affinity with the oxygen donor atom (Ren et al., 2020), so Fe3+ and ILB-CQDs surface groups (hydroxyl and aldehyde groups) are coordinated, which is also verified by the reduction of hydroxyl and aldehyde groups as seen in the four panels of Fig. 2. and S2. (b) and (c), in addition to the 288.6 eV at The enhanced signal is attributed to the increase of oxygen-containing carbon atoms (CO, C-O), which also indicates that more carboxyl groups are anchored on the surface of ILB-CQDs (Zhu et al., 2013). There is also a special coordination interaction between Fe3+ and the imide groups (Xinyun Zhai et al., 2012) and cyano (Schwab, 2011) in ILB-CQDs, and observation of both Fig. 2. and S2. (c) shows that the production of imide groups and nitrate makes the nitrogen-containing groups more. This indicated that the surface groups of ILB-CQDs had some chelating effect in the reaction with Fe3+ (Kong et al., 2012), which reduced the formation of nonradiative electron transfer or energy transfer (Zhu et al., 2012), and not only retained the carboxyl group (Liu et al., 2012a) but also greatly enhanced the N content and produced more nitrogen-containing groups, which led to enhanced fluorescence (Li et al., 2014).

The mechanism of Pd2+ detection by ILB-CQDs was investigated by XPS and FT-IR, and it can be seen from S3. that Pd2+ was successfully introduced into ILB-CQDs, and then the strong interaction between Pd2+ and the surface groups of ILB-CQDs reduced the radiative leap from the electron to the ground state and completed the separation of the electron-hole pairs, as seen from the XPS spectra of Fig. 2. and S3. shows that the nitrogen-containing groups are significantly reduced before and after the reaction with the addition of Pd2+. Moreover, Pd2+ also has an affinity with the oxygen donor atom, so the coordination of Pd2+ and ILB CQDs surface groups occurs, which can also be verified by the production of PdO seen in the four plates of Fig. 2. It is shown that Pd2+ binds to oxygen-containing groups in addition to complexation with alkyl groups, etc. This quench occurs through the formation of non-fluorescent complexes of Pd2+ and ILB-CQDs.

In the sensitivity study, different concentrations of Fe3+ and Pd2+ were investigated, and Fig. 8 (a and b) shows the photoluminescence spectra of ILB-CQDs at different concentrations, indicating that the fluorescence intensity of carbon quantum dots is sensitive to both Fe3+ and Pd2+, with the difference that the fluorescence detection of Fe3+ increases with increasing concentration and the opposite for fluorescence detection of Pd2+. The relationship between ILB-CQDs, Fe3+, and Pd2+ was investigated by UV and fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 4 and Fig. 8.).

It was found that the addition of Fe3+ caused a significant increase in the absorption peaks in the UV–vis spectra. The results suggest a synergistic effect between ILB-CQDs and Fe3+. XPS and IR spectra also demonstrate again the complex reaction between the surface groups of ILB-CQDs and Fe3+. Thus, Fe3+ caused fluorescence enhancement. This implies that the fluorescence enhancement of ILB-CQDs induced by Fe3+ is an enhancement mechanism opposite to the quenching mechanism. The dynamic fluorescence enhancement involves the transfer of electrons from the ground state to the excited state of ILB-CQDs, which further proves that the enhancement process of Fe3+ on ILB-CQDs is an effective electron transfer process, forming a new electron-hole separation process, and the transfer of excited state electrons of Fe3+ to the empty d orbitals of ILB-CQDs leads to the fluorescence enhancement effect, which also corresponds to the results of the fluorescence spectra of verification.

An increase in absorbance is also seen for the addition of Pd2+, which indicates that a stable non-fluorescent complex is generated between Pd2+ and ILB-CQDs surface groups (Shamsipur et al., 2018), which is also confirmed by XPS spectroscopy, where ILB-CQDs surface groups are ligated with Pd2+ and the positive charge is protected by Pd2+ (Qian et al., 2013). The presence of Pd2+ also prevents the ILB-CQDs surface groups from protonation to the amine group (Wang et al., 2017), leading to a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity. The complexes undergo aggregation and become black precipitates again, extracting Pd2+ and allowing the restoration of water bodies. As shown in Table 3, which lists the different detection methods and raw materials for Pd2+ detection by CQDs, ILB-CQDs have better detection limits. Moreover, in these assay applications, ILB-CQDs played an extractive role along with quench detection.

The inset of Fig. 8. (a) also shows the correlation between F/F0 and Fe3+ concentration, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of ILB-CQDs at 348 nm in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively. The photoluminescence quench was then analyzed using a computational formula (Wen et al., 2016), which first conforms to a nonlinear relationship in the range of 0–40 μmol/L, defined as F/F0 = 0.0346x+ 1.1162, with a correlation coefficient squared (R2) of 0.9944 and a detection limit of 0.001 nmol/L level. More comparing different methods and carbon sources for Fe3+ detection, it can be found that this study reduced the detection limit to 0.001 nmol/L, which is also a very low detection limit at the nmol/L level. For Pd2+ the detection limit is also shown in Fig. 8. (b), in the linear range of 0–20 μmol/L, the equation is defined as F/F0 = 0.1182x+ 1.0549, the correlation coefficient squared (R2) is 0.9912, and the detection limit is 0.23 μM. This detection limit is well below the World Health Organization's permissible threshold for palladium (47–94 μmol/L) (Haines, 1996). As shown in Table 4, which lists the different detection methods and raw materials for Fe3+ detection, ILB-CQDs lowered the detection limit down to the nmol/L level, while most of the detection limits are at the μmol/L level. And in the assay method where all of them are quench tables, the detection results are expressed as an enhancement.

For the recovery of ILB-CQDs for Fe3+ detection, theoretical analysis, and experimental validation were also performed. With the addition of pH buffer solution, phosphate, and Fe3+ exhibit affinity, and the two react chemically to produce iron phosphate co-precipitation, the bright yellow precipitate produced after the addition of pH buffer solution (Simmons, 2009). In this case, the oxygenated anion of phosphate interacts with the surface groups of the ILB-CQDs solution with the addition of Fe3+ through ligand exchange(PER PERSSON, 1995.5.1), PO4 3- (Anderson, 1989.5.26) interacts with Fe3+ to form Fe-O-P bonds with high affinity, so PO4 3- forms a stable complex with Fe3+ and precipitates bright yellow FePO4 solid. And the groups interacting with Fe3+ returned to the surface of ILB-CQDs, leading to a reduction of nitrogen-containing groups and a decrease in nitrogen content, and the fluorescence quenched by Fe3+ recovered to more than 90%. Unlike the extraction of Fe3+, the recovery of Pd2+ does not require the addition of other reagents, and the ILB-CQDs amine groups will form a stable black precipitate with Pd2+, thus the restoration of water bodies and achieving a recovery rate of more than 90%.

The next detection and extraction experiments for Fe3+ and Pd2+ were verified in real water samples. The water samples were taken from the real water samples of MeiXing. Different concentrations of Fe3+ and Pd2+ were added for mixing, and then the recoveries were calculated based on the fluorescence emission intensity. The water restoration rate of all samples was found to be above 90%.

The catalytic effect of ILs on biomass in this study resulted in ILB-CQDs with a uniformly dispersed spherical structure and a particle size distribution concentrated between 3 and 4 nm. This allowed ILB-CQDs to reach a stability period of more than 90 d with a QY of 26.7%. It has good stability at different pH values. The material has good selectivity in pure and actual water, and detects Pd2+ and Fe3+ by both quenching and brightening, respectively. It has a detection limit of 0.001 nM for Fe3+ and 0.23 µM for Pd2+ in pure water. The detection limits of Fe3+ in real water were 3.2 nmol/L and 0.36 µmol/L for Pd2+, both of which met the requirements of WHO drinking water standards. The material can not only detect Fe3+ and Pd2+, but also generate metal ion precipitation, which can have some purification effect on water bodies. And all of them can be extracted, and the extraction rate reaches more than 90%.

Kaimng Kang: Methodology, Software, Validation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Visualization.
